prompt: If photosynthesis had evolved to release chlorine gas instead of oxygen, how would Earth's biosphere plausibly differ today? Think it through; don't just list bullet points.
response:
Imagine that, very early in the history of life, the photochemical trick that splits water had taken one electron-transfer step in a slightly different direction. Instead of handing the stripped protons to a carrier that ultimately lets them recombine with the waste electrons to make O₂, the ancestral reaction center passed the same protons to chloride ions already abundant in Archean seawater. The by-product is Cl₂ gas, not O₂. Once that biochemical path was locked in, everything that followed had to cope with an atmosphere that slowly accumulated molecular chlorine instead of molecular oxygen.

First, the atmosphere itself would never have reached the high O₂ levels we know. Chlorine is so reactive that it would have been titrated out of the air almost as fast as it was produced. Most of it would have dissolved in the oceans, forming a planet-spanning, mildly acidic brine rich in hypochlorous acid and chloride. The air itself would probably stabilize at only a few parts per million of Cl₂—still enough to smell like a swimming pool and to bleach any organic surface exposed to sunlight, but not enough to co-exist with the long-lived O₂ reservoir that powers aerobic metabolism. In effect, Earth would have traded a redox buffer based on O₂/H₂O for one based on Cl₂/Cl⁻.

With no rising pO₂, the Great Oxidation Event never happens. Iron stays dissolved in the oceans, so the banded iron formations that color Precambrian geology would be absent. Methane would remain abundant, because the hydroxyl radical chemistry that normally breaks CH₄ apart in an oxygen-rich stratosphere is replaced by chlorine radical chemistry. The greenhouse effect of methane would keep Earth warmer than it otherwise would have been, perhaps delaying or preventing the Huronian glaciations. The sky would have a faint greenish tint from chlorine absorption, and the smell of the outdoors would be sharp and acrid.

Life would have to solve two problems simultaneously: harvesting light energy while surviving its own exhaust. The earliest photosynthetic mats would be layered like stromatolites, but the top millimeter would be a bleached, scorched zone where chlorine concentrations are lethal. Only organisms that secrete thick, chlorine-resistant slime—perhaps made of halogenated polymers similar to PVC—could survive in the sunlit layer. Below them, a dense undermat of dechlorinators would live on the chlorine diffusing downward, reducing Cl₂ back to Cl⁻ and gaining the small but usable redox energy that reaction provides. These dechlorinators would be Earth’s first “respirers,” analogous to our aerobic microbes, but running on Cl₂ instead of O₂.

Multicellularity could still evolve, but it would favor sealed, waxy, or rubbery integuments impervious to chlorine. Plants, if we can still call them that, would look more like bloated, translucent bladders or opaque, jointed
